case_name: Regina v. Institutional Head of Beaver Creek Correctional Camp, Ex parte MacCaud
court: ONTARIO [COURT OF APPEAL] KELLY, EVANS and JESSUP, JJ.A.
body_html:
18th NOVEMBER 1968.

Certiorari -- Availability -- Discipline of inmate by institutional head of penitentiary -- Whether certiorari available to inmate.

Prisons -- Inmate's rights -- Discipline of inmate by head of penitentiary -- Whether reviewable by certiorari -- Whether Canadian Bill of Rights applicable.

The availability of certiorari with respect to the exercise of any particular power is to be determined by the nature of the power itself and not the nature of the office held by the person exercising it since certiorari lies to supervise the discharge of authority by a body or person empowered to affect the civil rights of the citizen and required to act judicially. Although the major commitment of an institutional head of a penitentiary under the Penitentiary Act, 1962 (Can.), c. 53, is to make administrative decisions for which he is responsible to his superiors only and in regard to which the right of review by certiorari is not available, it is not impossible that the scope of his office may require him to make decisions, the character of which requires that he act in a judicial manner because such decisions affect the civil rights of the inmate. Whether the proceedings sought to be reviewed have deprived the inmate wholly or in part of his civil rights depends on whether they affect his status as a person as distinguished from his status as an inmate.

Under s. 29(1) of the Penitentiary Act the power to make Regulations is conferred on the Governor in Council and is denied to the Commissioner whose rule-making power is restricted to the making of rule known as Commissioner's directives. Directives made pursuant to such power do not confer statutory rights on an inmate affected by them in the way that a Regulation made by the Governor in Council would, and must be considered to be merely part of the administrative process for which the Commissioner is responsible. The duty of a staff member, including the institutional head, to adhere to the Commissioner's directives is a duty owed not to an inmate but rather to the staff member's superior. Non-observance of a directive does not adversely affect the jurisdiction of the institutional head in a disciplinary matter in respect of an inmate. With relation to an inmate, the institutional head's duty to act judicially in a matter in which he is required to observe the basic principles of fundamental justice involves an obligation to inform an inmate of the disciplinary offence he is alleged to have committed, to give him a fair opportunity to present his case and the evidence relevant to the matters he is called upon to face, and to reach his decision judicially upon the material before him and not capriciously or in reliance upon some consideration not relevant to the charge. Having regard to the nature of this obligation of the institutional head and to the fact that it is only where his action does not affect the rights of the inmate as a person, or his statutory rights as an inmate, the head is not answerable to the Court for the propriety of his procedures and the legality of his decision, the Canadian Bill of Rights, 1960 (Can.), c. 44, confers no broader rights on an inmate than those conferred on him by the Court's interpretation of the Penitentiary Act and the Regulations under it.

[Ridge v. Baldwin, [1964] A.C. 40, refd to]

APPEAL by an inmate of a penitentiary against a dismissal of his application for an order by way of certiorari with respect to disciplinary action taken against him by the head of the penitentiary.

Claude R. Thomson, for appellant.

N.A. Chalmers, for the Crown, respondent.

BY THE COURT:-- The applicant, who had been regularly convicted and sentenced to imprisonment for a period of two years or more, is serving his sentence in a penitentiary established and conducted pursuant to the provisions of the Penitentiary Act, 1960-61 (Can.), c. 53 (the Act). An application was made by him for an order by way of certiorari with respect to a disciplinary action taken by the superintendent of the Beaver Creek Correctional Camp, designated as a minimum security institution.

In the proceedings in the Court below, the applicant was not represented by counsel. On the dismissal of such application the appellant, through counsel, appealed to this Court limiting his appeal to a single ground, i.e., that the learned Judge of first instance erred in dismissing the application on the grounds that the institutional head of the penitentiary acting in his disciplinary capacity was not amenable to certiorari.

Since the facts concerning the disciplinary action and the conduct in respect of which it was purportedly exercised, are not before the Court, the sole question which counsel for the applicant asked the Court to decide was the following:

Is an Institutional Head, acting in his disciplinary capacity, amendable to certiorari if he acts without jurisdiction?

In the somewhat bald terms in which the question is phrased there seems to be an invitation to give an equally terse answer to the effect that there is not before the Court sufficient particulars of the act of the institutional head to enable the Court to frame an answer. However, since the present application is but one of many of a somewhat similar nature launched by the appellant, and since counsel for the appellant in his statement of points of law and fact has stated the specific complaints of the appellant in respect of which the present application was launched, we have felt it desirable to consider generally the implications of an institutional head acting with respect to discipline and imposing sanctions for infractions of discipline.

The complaints of the appellant, as set out by his counsel in the statement of points of law and fact, include the following:

1. That he was denied a hearing;

2. That he was denied the right to give evidence;

3. That he was not told the charges against him;

4. That the punishment imposed was not one authorized by law.

The authority of a Court or judicial officer to impose imprisonment as a punishment for the commission of a crime flows from the Criminal Code, which also provides that all sentences of two years or more shall be served in one of the penitentiaries, for the maintenance and operation of which provision is made by the Act.

The chief officer of the Penitentiary Service is the Commissioner appointed by the Governor in Council: he, under the direction of the Minister, has the control and management of the Penitentiary Service and all matters connected therewith (s. 9). In the absence of or inability to act of the Commissioner, the Deputy Commissioner, also appointed by the Governor in Council, or him failing, the Acting Commissioner, acts in the place and stead of the Commissioner.

Directors of Divisions and Regional Directors may be appointed by the Minister; all other officers and employees necessary for the administration of the Penitentiary Act, including institutional heads, are appointed by the Commissioner under the direction of the Minister.

The Act contains no specifications of the responsibility of any of the officers of the Service other than the Commissioner, Deputy Commissioner or Acting Commissioner; save with respect to certain provisions dealing with statutory remission to which reference will be made later, the Act is silent as to discipline of inmates. Section 29(1) confers on the Governor in Council power to make regulations,

(a) for the organization, training, discipline, efficiency, administration and good government of the Service;

(b) for the custody, treatment, training, employment and discipline of inmates; and

(c) generally, for carrying into effect the purposes and provisions of this Act. Pursuant to s. 29(1) the Penitentiary Service Regulations were made by the Governor in Council on March 8, 1962, to become effective on April 1, 1962, and in compliance with the requirements of the Regulations Act, R.S.C. 1952, c. 235, were published in 96 Can. Gaz. on March 28, 1962, p. 295, as P.C. 1962-302, SOR/62-90.

In Part II of the Regulations headed "Custody and Training of Inmates" appear ss. 2.27, 2.28, 2.29 and 2.30 which are the only sections pertinent to the matters at issue in this appeal. The text of these sections is set out in Schedule "A".

Section 29(3) confers upon the Commissioner power to make directives in the following terms:

29(3) Subject to this Act and any regulations made under subsection (1), the Commissioner may make rules, to be known as Commissioner's directives, for the organization, training, discipline, efficiency, administration and good government of the Service, and for the custody, treatment, training, employment and discipline of inmates and the good government of penitentiaries.

The Court was informed by counsel for the Attorney-General of Canada that there was in existence "Commissioner's Directive No. 300 dated 20th September, 1963". The text of pertinents parts of this directive is set out in Schedule "B". This directive which was made known and circulated amongst the members of the Service affected by it was not published or communicated to the inmates. It appears, however, that the appellant has become aware of the contents of the directive otherwise than through authorized channels.

The exercise by the institutional head of the power to maintain discipline is inherent in the duty imposed upon him by the Regulations, s. 2.28(1), which reads:

2.28(1) The institutional head of each institution is responsible for the disciplinary control of inmates confined therein.

The question before the Court relates to the exercise of such power.

At the outset it is necessary briefly to consider the basis of the remedy of certiorari. Originally, the issuance of the writ of certiorari was the means by which the Court of King's Bench, of which the Supreme Court of Ontario is the successor, exercised a supervision over the proceedings of inferior Courts in matters other than the liberty of the subject, for the interference with which habeas corpus appears to have been, and to be, the appropriate remedy. The use of certiorari is not now confined to the supervision of Courts but has been extended to the exercise of authority by any body empowered to affect the civil rights of the citizen. From the power to affect such civil rights is to be inferred the duty of such a body to act judicially and if it has such a duty the Supreme Court of Ontario will supervise the discharge of such duty. Thus in deciding whether certiorari lies to call up for review a proceeding of any body, the determination as to whether a particular proceeding is a judicial one must be made not with reference to the nature of the character of the tribunal but with reference to the power purported to be exercised. Where the body whose proceedings are sought to be reviewed has the duty to act judicially by virtue of the power it exercises, it must act in conformity with any lawful provisions binding upon it which place any limit upon the jurisdiction which it may exercise. In addition, unless by clear and unambiguous words jurisdiction is conferred upon it to act in a manner which would otherwise be an infringement of the principles of natural justice, the Courts will assume that the Legislature intends that, in the exercise of the judicial jurisdiction conferred on it, it will observe the requirements of natural justice. Accordingly, any departure from the principles of natural justice, the observance of which is not specifically exempted, any purported exercise of a jurisdiction not conferred, or any exercise of such jurisdiction in excess of the limits lawfully imposed will be grounds on which the exercise of powers requiring a body to act judicially are subject to supervisory review through an application to the Court by way of certiorari.

Having in mind Lord Reid's warning in Ridge v. Baldwin, [1964] A.C. 40 at p. 65, as to the need to be aware of the differences between various classes of cases, attention must be given to the nature of the relationship between the institutional head and the inmates of the institution, but in the last analysis the measure by which the availability of certiorari with respect to the exercise of any particular power is to be determined, must be the nature of the power itself and not the nature of the office held by the person exercising it.

Since the nature of the relationship between an institutional head and inmate differs essentially from other relationships dealt with in cases to which counsel referred, little assistance is to be had from them, except to the extent that they lay down basic principles of universal application. For this reason we do not propose to review in detail cases where the relief sought was with respect to the exercise of some power allegedly judicial, affecting the rights arising out of an employer-employee relationship; nor cases where the applicant complained of an action affecting him, of some professional or voluntary association of which he was a member; nor to cases dealing with the right to review Courts Martial where the element of voluntary submission to an internal code seems to have been the determining factor, as it has been to a great extent in cases dealing with police and fire department; nor do we find any similarity in the nexus of teacher to pupil, since this is an extension of the relationship of the parent to the child.

The commission of a crime, its discovery, the prosecution of the offender, his conviction and sentence, constitute a predictable succession dependent for its initiation on the voluntary act of the prisoner. Since imprisonment in a penitentiary is impossible without there having been such a voluntary act, it is strictly correct to say that the confinement of an inmate in a penitentiary is the result of a voluntary choice on his part. Nevertheless, it would be unrealistic to assume that the prisoner's conformity to the regime of the institution would result from his spontaneous co-operation; the authority of the institutional staff to enforce discipline and its readiness to take the measures necessary to ensure the observance of standards of conduct required of inmates must be regarded as the foundation for the orderly conduct of the institution.

It is clear that in the performance of his duties as an officer of the Service in charge of an institution and responsible for the safe custody and disciplinary control of its inmates, the major commitment of an institutional head is to make administrative decisions for which he is responsible to his superiors only and in regard to which the right of review by certiorari is not available. However, this fact does not preclude the possibility that the scope of his office may require him to make decisions, the character of which requires that he act in a judicial manner because such decisions may affect the civil rights of the inmate. The problem lies in identifying the circumstances which add to the administrative quality of the act he is performing in the discharge of his duty, the quality of a power to make decisions the nature of which requires that he act therein judicially. The proper test to be applied is to ask whether the proceedings sought to be reviewed have deprived the inmate wholly or in part of his civil rights in that they affect his status as a person as distinguished from his status as an inmate. If the application of this test provides an affirmative answer, in arriving at that decision the institutional head is performing a "judicial" act.

It would be trite to say that an inmate of an institution continues to enjoy all the civil rights of a person save those that are taken away or interfered with by his having been lawfully sentenced to imprisonment. Rather we consider that it is desirable to attempt to enumerate what are the civil rights to which an inmate remains entitled, which may be affected by the act of the institutional head of the penitentiary in which he is an inmate.

At the outset, it must be observed that the passing of a sentence upon a convicted criminal extinguishes, for the period of his lawful confinement, all his rights to liberty and to the personal possession of property within the institution in which he is confined, save to the extent, if any, that those rights are expressly preserved by the Penitentiary Act. Since his right to liberty is for the time being non-existent, all decisions of the officers of the Penitentiary Service with respect to the place and manner of confinement are the exercise of an authority which is purely administrative, provided that such decisions do not otherwise transgress rights conferred or preserved by the Penitentiary Act. Likewise, the withdrawal of or restrictive interference with privileges, the normal punishment for a disciplinary offence which is not flagrant or serious, does not affect any civil right of the inmate as a person: and if the exercise of the disciplinary powers inherent in the administrative functions of the institutional head results only in the withdrawal of privileges, this is not the exercise of a power which so affects the civil rights of the prisoner as a person as to endow the withdrawal or interference with the character of a judicial act. Of a similar nature we consider the crediting or abstaining from crediting remission which is defined as "earned remission" to be a purely discretionary administrative act on the part of the officers of the Service.

On the other hand, forfeiture of statutory remission as a penalty would entail the prolongation of the period of confinement beyond the time for which the inmate has been sentenced, less the statutory remission with which he is entitled to be credited; to that extent it would affect his liberty and a decision to forfeit such remission would accordingly be reviewable on certiorari.

The ordering of corporal punishment which is punishment inflicted upon the person, e.g., strapping as opposed to punishment of the person, e.g., alteration of the locale or nature of confinement, does affect the civil rights of an inmate to personal security. Therefore, the decision to award corporal punishment is the exercise of a power by its nature judicial.

Co-relative to the suspension of some of his civil rights, in his special status as an inmate, an inmate becomes entitled to certain statutory civil rights which, largely because they are inapplicable to a person other than an inmate, are not within the usual category of civil rights of the individual. In dealing with the disregard of or infringement upon the inmate's statutory rights as an inmate, it is first necessary to consider what are those statutory rights. The rights conferred by statute we take to be those included in statutory enactments and in Regulations made by the Governor in Council: and these appear to be the Penitentiary Act and the Regulations made pursuant to s. 29(1). These Regulations having been made pursuant to the authority conferred by the Act upon the Governor in Council, and duly published, have the same force as law, as have the provisions of the statute itself. It was submitted by counsel for the appellant that the Commissioner's directives, made pursuant to the power conferred on the Commissioner by s. 29(3), also had statutory force equivalent to the Regulations made pursuant to the Act and that accordingly they conferred statutory rights on the inmate. In our view, to give the directives such force would be to invest them with a status not contemplated by the statute. It is significant that the power of making Regulations is denied to the Commissioner but is expressly reserved to the Governor in Council by s. 29(1). The Regulations so made constitute, with the statute, the legislative framework in which the scope of the Penitentiary Act is defined and determined; the Regulations complement and particularize the statute and together with it make up the legal requirements within and in compliance with which the Penitentiary Service is created and operates. The Commissioner to whom under the direction of the Minister is committed the control and management of the Service, as has been pointed out, is primarily an executive officer and as such the directives he makes pursuant to s. 29(3) are part of the administrative process for which he is responsible. The Commissioner for his assistance has a large force, geographically widely dispersed, by means of which he discharges the duties of his office. He must, of necessity, carry to those under his direction, in some form more lasting than word of mouth, the manner in which each member of the staff is expected to conduct himself in the discharge of the duties which are relevant to the post he holds. His directives, which are internal to the Penitentiary Service, may and probably do govern the employer-employee relationship between the staff member and his superiors as part of the administrative structure. They define for the staff member the manner in which, and the limits within which, he and other members of this service are expected to perform their duties; departure from the directives may constitute an infraction of the obligation owed by the staff member to his superior, but any conduct on the part of a staff member which, in the absence of the directives, would not constitute an infringement of some civil right or right conferred on the inmate by the statute and Regulations, does not by virtue of the directives become such an infringement. In other words, there is no obligation owed by a staff member to the inmate to adhere to the directives. The duty owed by the staff member to the inmate must be found in the statute and Regulations.

We are therefore of the opinion that the non-observance of the provisions of a directive made under s. 29(3) would not in itself have any debilitating effect on the jurisdiction which the institutional head is exercising in a disciplinary matter. Non-observance of a directive, while a breach of obedience owed by the institutional head to the Commissioner, does not result of itself in a lack of jurisdiction or constitute an excess of jurisdiction while he is acting under the authority spelled out in the Act and Regulations.

In holding that in a matter in which he is required to act judicially the institutional head shall observe the basic principles of fundamental justice, it is our opinion that this requires that the inmate affected be fully informed of the disciplinary offence he is alleged to have committed, that he be given a fair opportunity to present his case and the evidence relevant to the matters he is called upon to face, and that the decision of the institutional head be arrived at judicially, upon the material properly before him and not capriciously or in reliance upon some consideration not relevant to the charge.

It was finally submitted in the argument of counsel for the appellant that the Canadian Bill of Rights, 1960 (Can.), c. 44, was relevant because in its provisions there was a prohibition against the deprivation of the rights of a person to a fair hearing, in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice, for the determination of his rights and obligations.

As interpreted in this judgment, the laws of Canada do not deprive the inmate of a fair hearing in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice on any matter relating to the determination of his "rights and obligations". We have pointed out that where the civil rights of an inmate, as we construe those rights to be, may be affected by the decision of an institutional head, there must be a fair hearing in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice. It is only where the action of the institutional head does not affect the rights of the inmate as a person, or his statutory rights as an inmate, that the institutional head is not answerable to the Court for the propriety of his procedures and the legality of his decision. To put it in other words, the interpretation placed on the Penitentiary Act and the Regulations under it are as restrictive upon the actions of an institutional head as are the provisions of the Canadian Bill of Rights.

To recapitulate, certiorari with respect to the exercise by an institutional head of a penitentiary of his disciplinary jurisdiction will not lie unless:

(a) the procedure sought to be reviewed is one in which the institutional head was required, by reason of the power exercised by him, to act judicially as well as administratively; and

(b) (1) the action sought to be reviewed was in excess of any jurisdiction lawfully exercisable by the institutional head; or

(2) there was a complete absence of any jurisdiction to take the action complained of, or

(3) in the exercise of the jurisdiction lawfully conferred upon him, the institutional head did not observe the principles of fundamental justice applicable to the proceedings.

Since the material before the Court does not reveal the particular decision made by the institutional head sought to be reviewed in these proceedings, this matter must be referred back to the Court of first instance for reargument and for reconsideration in the light of the principles above outlined: the applicant and respondent should be at liberty to put before the Court in the usual manner further material otherwise admissible if counsel so advise.

Although we would point out that the application made by the appellant was a civil, not a criminal proceeding, and it lay within the discretion of the Court to direct the payment of costs by the appellant, in this matter the Court does not see fit to make any order as to costs.

SCHEDULE "A"

Extract from Regulation made by Governor in Council pursuant to s. 29(1) of the Penitentiary Act.

2.27. It is the duty of the institutional head to take all reasonable steps to ensure the safe custody of inmates committed to his care.

2.28(1) The institutional head of each institution is responsible for the disciplinary control of inmates confined therein.

(2) No inmate shall be punished except pursuant to the order of the institutional head or his lawful deputy.

(3) Where an inmate is convicted of a disciplinary offence, the punishment shall, except where the offence is flagrant or serious, consist of loss of privileges.

(4) The punishment that may be ordered for a flagrant or serious disciplinary offence shall consist of any one or more of the following, namely,

(a) forfeiture of statutory remission,

(b) dissociation for a period not to exceed thirty days with a diet, during all or part of the period, that is monotonous but adequate and healthful, or

(c) corporal punishment, not exceeding fifteen strokes of the strap.

2.29 Every inmate commits a disciplinary offence who

(a) disobeys or fails to obey a lawful order of a penitentiary officer,

(b) assaults or threatens to assault another person,

(c) refuses to work or fails to work to the best of his ability,

(d) leaves his work without permission of a penitentiary officer,

(e) damages government property or the property of another person,

(f) wilfully wastes food,

(g) is indecent, disrespectful or threatening in his actions, language or writing toward any other person,

(h) wilfully disobeys or fails to obey any regulation or rule governing the conduct of inmates,

(i) has contraband in his possession,

(j) deals in contraband with any other person,

(k) does any act that is calculated to prejudice the discipline or good order of the institution,

(l) does any act with intent to escape or to assist another inmate to escape,

(m) gives or offers a bribe or reward to any person for any purpose,

(n) contravenes any rule, regulation or directive made under the Act, or

(o) attempts to do anything mentioned in paragraphs (a) to (n).

2.30(1) Where the institutional head is satisfied that

(a) for the maintenance of good order and discipline in the institution, or

(b) in the best interests of an inmate

it is necessary or desirable that the inmate should be kept from associating with other inmates he may order the inmate to be dissociated accordingly, but the case of every inmate so dissociated shall be considered, not less than once each month, by the Classification Board for the purpose of recommending to the institutional head whether or not the inmate should return to association with other inmates.

(2) An inmate who has been dissociated is not considered under punishment unless he has been sentenced as such and he shall not be deprived of any of his privileges and amenities by reason thereof, except those privileges and amenities that

(a) can only be enjoyed in association with other inmates, or

(b) cannot reasonably be granted having regard to the limitations of the dissociation area and the necessity for the effective operation thereof.

SCHEDULE "B"

Commissioner's Directive No. 300 dated 20th September, 1963. Inmate Discipline

Inmate Disciplinary Board

1. (1) There shall be, at each institution, an inmate disciplinary board consisting of three members.

(2) The chairman of the board shall be the deputy head, in whose absence the institutional head shall act as chairman of the board.

(3) Except where otherwise provided, the members of the board shall be the assistant deputy wardens and such other officers as the institutional head may designate from time to time.

(4) At minimum security institutions, the institutional head shall be the chairman of the board and he shall designate the members of the board.

(5) The institutional head shall appoint an appropriate officer to serve permanently as clerk to the board.

(6) The board shall sit daily or as otherwise required.

(7) Notwithstanding (2) above, in any case where the institutional head considers it necessary or advisable that he shall assume the duties of chairman of the Inmate Disciplinary Board, it is his prerogative to do so.

Selection of Tribunal

2. Where an inmate is charged with a disciplinary offence the chairman of the disciplinary board, after consultation with the institutional head, shall determine whether the allegation, if proved, would constitute a flagrant or serious disciplinary offence meriting punishment by way of forfeiture of statutory remission, punitive dissociation or corporal punishment and, if he so determines, shall have the case heard by the disciplinary board; but if he does not so determine he shall hear the case by himself.

Hearing of Charges

3. (1) No finding or award of punishment shall be made against an inmate unless he:

(a) has received notice of the charge against him;

(b) has appeared personally at the hearing, and

(c) has been given an opportunity to question witnesses, make a statement, and, generally, make his full answer and defence to the charge.

(2) The hearing of a charge against an inmate shall be disposed of with the least possible delay but may, when circumstances require, be adjourned from time to time.

(3) Disciplinary court procedure shall be in accordance with divisional staff instructions.

(4) A written record of the proceedings shall be kept by the clerk to the board on form as per Schedule #300 -- "A".

Divisional Staff Instructions 300.02 Inmate Discipline

Disciplinary Court

1. (1) The chairman of the inmate disciplinary board shall inquire into every report made against an inmate and shall review each such report with the institutional head.

(2) If the report so justified, the chairman of the board shall cause a lawful charge to be laid against the inmate who shall be escorted without undue delay to the disciplinary court, where the charge will be read to him.

(3)(a) If the inmate pleads guilty to the charge, the disciplinary board shall familiarize itself with the facts surrounding the offence.

(b) If the inmate pleads not guilty, the officer making the report, or the complainant, or any other necessary witness, shall give testimony under oath, and the evidence shall be given in the presence of the inmate who shall be given the opportunity to question witnesses, through the chairman of the board.

(4) If an inmate pleads guilty or, if after a plea of not guilty, a disciplinary offence is proved, the disciplinary board or the chairman of the board shall award such punishment within the scope of its authority as such offence may justify.

(5) No punishment or deprivation of any kind shall be awarded to an inmate by any penitentiary officer except the disciplinary board or its chairman. This instruction shall in no way be construed to prevent an officer from confining, restraining, or temporarily depriving an inmate of any right or privilege until he is brought before the disciplinary board or other appropriate action is taken.

Order accordingly.
